problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem B (discrete) "Reservoir Management" on the Colorado River's Glen Canyon Dam (Lake Powell) and Hoover Dam (Lake Mead). The paper must: (Task 1) decide how much water to draw daily from each lake to meet agriculture/industry/residential/hydropower demand for 5 states (AZ, CA, WY, NM, CO), estimate how many days until demand can no longer be met with no inflow, the additional daily water needed, account for Mexico's downstream share, and recommend how often the model should be re-run; (Task 2) balance the competing uses of general consumption vs. electricity generation subject to a hydropower lower bound; (Task 3) propose a priority-based allocation when water is scarce and demand cannot be fully met; and perform sensitivity/robustness analysis. The winning team built a coupled "Double-Dam" two-node difference-equation system feeding an optimization layer, a multi-objective genetic-algorithm allocator, and a PCA-based state/use importance ranking for scarcity.
models_used: Double-Dam coupled linear difference-equation system: two state sequences {a_n} (Lake Powell/Glen Canyon volume) and {b_n} (Lake Mead/Hoover volume), with outflow coefficients k13/k14 (Powell to general use / power) and k23/k24 (Mead to general use / power), upstream inflow e constant, and Powell->Mead coupling via k14. Closed-form general solution derived by matrix diagonalization (eigenvalues alpha, beta of M=[[A,B],[D,E]]) after homogenizing the nonhomogeneous recurrence

Demand-estimation sub-models converting public data into water-volume demand: D_e (hydropower) from per-capita electricity x population x 6.98% hydro share x 3.67 m3/kWh; D_a (agriculture) from farmland acres x 364.50 m3/acre; D_i (industry) from laborers x per-capita water expense / water tariff; D_r (residential) from population x 0.54 m3/capita
Task 1 optimization model: minimize |D_g - a_n*k13 - b_n*k23| and |D_e - a_n*k14 - b_n*k24| (minimize supply-demand mismatch / waste) subject to D_e+D_g < V_m+V_p and 0<k13+k23<1, 0<k14+k24<1

Task 2 multi-objective coordination model: 'coordination level' U_A(delta, delta*) defined piecewise (=1 if supply ratio >= demand ratio, else Gaussian exp[-(delta-delta*)^2/S_A^2]); objective max f(x)=sqrt(U_E * U_G), the geometric mean of electricity and general-use coordination

Genetic Algorithm solving the Task 2 allocation over a 10-dim vector (x1-x5 electricity per state, x6-x10 general per state): population 200, fitness Fit(x)=f(x)+penalty*sum(cost_i - x_i), roulette-wheel selection, arithmetic crossover (Pc=0.8), mutation (Pm=0.1), 1000 generations, elitism (carry best individual forward)

Task 3 Principal Component Analysis (PCA): build 4x5 demand matrix A (electricity/agriculture/industry/daily-life x 5 states), standardize, compute covariance matrix Sigma, take eigenvector v of largest eigenvalue, set b=A*v, normalize to get distribution ratios per use and (after transposing A) per state
Sensitivity/robustness analysis using a 'satisfaction rate' = Supply/Demand metric across demand multipliers, water-saving (0.8) and renewable-energy (0.8) scenarios, and per-sector demand scaling
key_techniques: Abstraction of two physical reservoirs into a 2-node directed flow system (one-way Powell->Mead arrow + external inflow + outflows to general use, power, and Mexico), making the coupling between dams explicit and tractable

Analytic closed-form solution of the coupled recurrence via eigen-decomposition rather than only numerical iteration, giving a_n and b_n as explicit functions used directly inside the optimization objective

Data-to-demand pipeline: no demand data was given, so they engineered each demand from real public datasets (EIA energy data, USDA agriculture, Data USA) with named unit conversion constants (3.67 m3/kWh, 364.50 m3/acre, 0.54 m3/capita, 6.98% hydro share)
Penalty-function reformulation turning the constrained multi-objective allocation into a single fitness function the GA can optimize
Geometric-mean coordination objective sqrt(U_E*U_G) that forces BOTH uses to be satisfied (a low value in either factor tanks the product), elegantly encoding the 'competing uses' requirement
PCA repurposed as an importance/weighting tool for prioritization under scarcity rather than for dimensionality reduction
Discovery and reporting of an emergent periodic-rebound-within-decline pattern in storage, then converting it into an actionable recommendation: re-run the model every ~100 days
why_it_won: "It answered every sub-question of all three tasks with a concrete, defensible number (430M m3/day from Powell, 225M m3/day from Mead, demand met until day 61, 40.7M m3/day extra needed, ~150M m3 Powell->Mead transfer, ~100-day re-run cycle, CA gets ~50-67% of water, electricity ~50%+ of use) rather than hand-waving — judges reward specific, traceable answers. The Double-Dam abstraction is a genuinely elegant modeling choice: it captures the inter-dam coupling the problem hinges on, yet stays simple enough to solve in closed form and re-run quickly, which the team explicitly framed as a decision-support benefit for reservoir managers. The three tasks use three distinct, appropriate tools (difference-equation + optimization, multi-objective GA, PCA) showing methodological range without overreaching. They closed real data gaps with cited sources and explicit conversion constants, did a genuinely multi-scenario sensitivity analysis (demand multiples, water-saving, renewables, per-sector scaling, upstream inflow), and surfaced a non-obvious emergent insight (periodic self-regulation) that they turned into an operational recommendation. The full MATLAB code for both the optimization and GA is in the appendix, signaling reproducibility. Plus they nailed the format requirement most teams botch: a polished standalone journalistic memo ('Should Dams Be Condemned?') that reframes the technical results for a general audience and lands a clear policy message (blame climate change, not the dams)."
section_highlights: "Strong structure: tightly-scoped summary sheet that already states the headline numbers; explicit Problem Restatement that re-derives each sub-question so nothing is missed; numbered Assumptions each paired with a justification ('water level proportional to volume — because dam-terrain geometry is hard to model'); a clean symbol/abbreviation table; a full-work flowchart (Figure 2) mapping the entire pipeline before any math. Each task section follows a consistent Analyze -> Construct/Extend -> Solve -> Result rhythm with descriptive sub-headings ('The In and Out of Water'), making it skimmable. Figures carry the narrative: per-state data bar charts, the Double-Dam flow diagram, the GA flowchart, allocation bar/pie/donut charts, and a 4-panel sensitivity figure. Appendix delivers the recurrence-solution derivation, source references, raw result tables, and runnable MATLAB. The separate news-article memo with subheads, pull-imagery, and an 'Editor: Team #2211922' byline is a standout format flourish."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy reliance on assumed/looked-up conversion constants (3.67 m3/kWh, 364.50 m3/acre, 0.54 m3/capita, 6.98% hydro) with little uncertainty quantification on these inputs — the whole demand layer rests on them

Internal inconsistency and typos undercut rigor: the symbol table defines BOTH D_e and D_i as 'Water demand for power generation'; Task 3 donut chart lists Industry and Resident both at 37.65% (clearly an error); some numbers are dimensionally suspect (e.g. '40.7M m3/day ... about 2 trillion m3 within 61 days' — 40.7M x 61 is ~2.5 billion, not trillion); reservoir volumes (24-25 billion m3) vs daily draws are loosely reconciled

The constant-inflow assumption (e fixed) is acknowledged but is exactly what produces the 'periodic rebound' they tout as an insight — the periodicity is partly an artifact of the constant-forcing linear recurrence, not necessarily a real hydrological self-regulation
Rainfall omitted entirely and upstream inflow held constant, so the model cannot represent the actual stochastic drought dynamics central to the problem's premise

Task 3 PCA importance ranking conflates economic dominance with allocation priority (CA gets 60-67% purely because it's largest), which is a normative/equity choice presented as if objective — and CO collapses to 0.69%, an implausibly harsh result that isn't sanity-checked

GA fitness uses an un-normalized penalty sum with a single penalty factor and no reported convergence/validation of optimality; the appendix MATLAB has clear syntax errors (unbalanced parentheses in the bn expression), so the published code would not run as-is
Mexico's allocation is handled only loosely (capped at b_n*k24 'at most') rather than as a hard treaty constraint
reusable_patterns: The 'system abstraction' move: collapse coupled physical entities into a small directed-graph of nodes with transfer coefficients, write it as a coupled linear difference/ODE system — broadly reusable for any reservoir/inventory/compartment problem
Solve a coupled linear recurrence in closed form via homogenization + matrix diagonalization, then plug the analytic a_n/b_n straight into the optimization objective (avoids nested numerical loops)

Demand engineering from public data when the problem gives none: pick one authoritative dataset per sector (EIA, USDA, census), one named conversion constant per sector, and show the formula — defensible and fast

Geometric-mean (sqrt of product) objective to force ALL competing objectives to be jointly satisfied instead of trading off; pairs naturally with a piecewise 'coordination level' that saturates at 1 once demand is met
Penalty-function + GA as a general-purpose solver for constrained multi-objective allocation when the feasible region is messy
PCA-as-importance-weighting to derive priority/allocation ratios under scarcity from a feature x entity matrix
Mine the model's own output for an emergent qualitative pattern (here, periodic rebound) and convert it into an operational rule (re-run cadence) — adds insight beyond the asked numbers
Deliver the required non-paper artifact (memo/letter/news article) as a fully self-contained, audience-appropriate piece with its own headline and policy takeaway, not a recycled abstract
